chunk_summary: Guidelines for therapeutic vitamin A dosing in children with xerophthalmia or measles, emphasizing age-based regimens, follow-up, and management of side effects.
learning_objectives: Understand age-based therapeutic vitamin A dosing schedules for xerophthalmia and measles.
Recognize the importance of follow-up doses and caregiver education in vitamin A treatment.
Identify expected responses and manage side effects of high-dose vitamin A therapy.
Evaluate non-response cases and consider alternative diagnoses or malnutrition pathways.
key_facts: Therapeutic vitamin A dosing: 50,000 IU for infants <6 months, 100,000 IU for 6–12 months, and 200,000 IU for children >12 months.
Treatment schedule: Three doses on day 1, day 2, and day 14 for xerophthalmia; two consecutive days for measles.
Vitamin A is critical in measles care, especially for malnourished or severely ill children with eye signs.
Transient nausea or vomiting may occur after high-dose therapy but does not justify stopping treatment.
Non-response management includes verifying doses, assessing malabsorption, and enrolling in nutrition rehabilitation.
reference_quote: Vitamin A is also part of supportive care in measles because measles can precipitate deficiency and worsen ocular outcomes. (WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children)
document_key_topics: Therapeutic vitamin A dosing for xerophthalmia
Caregiver education and follow-up in treatment
